title: 5' cap

infobox.value: (**5′ cap**) is a specially altered [nucleotide](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") on the [5′ end](/wiki/Directionality_(molecular_biology)#5′-end "Directionality (molecular biology)") of some [primary transcripts](/wiki/Primary_transcript "Primary transcript") such as [precursor messenger RNA](/wiki/Precursor_mRNA "Precursor mRNA"). This process, known as **mRNA capping**, is highly regulated and vital in the creation of stable and [mature messenger RNA](/wiki/Mature_messenger_RNA "Mature messenger RNA") able to undergo [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") during [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_synthesis "Protein synthesis"). [Mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion") mRNA[[1]](#cite_note-temperley2010-1) and [chloroplastic](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast") mRNA[[2]](#cite_note-monde2000-2) are not capped.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [5' cap](/w/index.php?title=5%27_cap&redirect=no "5' cap"))

Specially altered nucleotide on the 5' end of pre-mRNA

In [molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology"), the **five-prime cap** (**5′ cap**) is a specially altered [nucleotide](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") on the [5′ end](/wiki/Directionality_(molecular_biology)#5′-end "Directionality (molecular biology)") of some [primary transcripts](/wiki/Primary_transcript "Primary transcript") such as [precursor messenger RNA](/wiki/Precursor_mRNA "Precursor mRNA"). This process, known as **mRNA capping**, is highly regulated and vital in the creation of stable and [mature messenger RNA](/wiki/Mature_messenger_RNA "Mature messenger RNA") able to undergo [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") during [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_synthesis "Protein synthesis"). [Mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion") mRNA[[1]](#cite_note-temperley2010-1) and [chloroplastic](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast") mRNA[[2]](#cite_note-monde2000-2) are not capped.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-prime_cap&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/5%27_cap_structure.png/250px-5%27_cap_structure.png)](/wiki/File:5%27_cap_structure.png)

5′ cap structure (cap-2).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Ribose_Structure_2.svg/250px-Ribose_Structure_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ribose_Structure_2.svg)

Ribose structure showing the positions of the 2′, 3′ and 5′ carbons.

In [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryotes "Eukaryotes"), the 5′ cap (cap-0), found on the 5′ end of an mRNA molecule, consists of a [guanine](/wiki/Guanine "Guanine") nucleotide connected to mRNA via an unusual 5′ to 5′ [triphosphate](/wiki/Triphosphate "Triphosphate") linkage. This [guanosine](/wiki/Guanosine "Guanosine") is [methylated](/wiki/Methylation "Methylation") on the 7 position directly after capping *in vivo* by a [methyltransferase](/wiki/Methyltransferase "Methyltransferase").[[3]](#cite_note-shatkin-3)[[4]](#cite_note-banerjee-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sonenberg-5)[[6]](#cite_note-marcotrigiano-6) It is referred to as a [7-methylguanylate](/wiki/7-Methylguanosine "7-Methylguanosine") cap, abbreviated m7G. The Cap-0 is the base cap structure, however, the first and second transcribed nucleotides can also be 2' O-methylated, leading to the Cap-1 and Cap-2 structures, respectively. This is more common in higher eukaryotes and thought to be part of the innate immune system to recognize mRNAs from other organisms.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In multicellular eukaryotes and some viruses,[[8]](#cite_note-fechter2005-8) further modifications can be made, including the methylation of the 2′ [hydroxy-groups](/wiki/Hydroxyl "Hydroxyl") of the first two [ribose](/wiki/Ribose "Ribose") sugars of the 5′ end of the mRNA. cap-1 has a methylated 2′-hydroxy group on the first ribose sugar, while cap-2 has methylated 2′-hydroxy groups on the first two ribose sugars, shown on the right. The 5′ cap is chemically similar to the [3′ end](/wiki/3%E2%80%B2_end "3′ end") of an RNA molecule (the 5′ carbon of the cap ribose is bonded, and the 3′ unbonded). This provides significant resistance to 5′ [exonucleases](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease").[[9]](#cite_note-Furuichi2015-9)

[Small nuclear RNAs](/wiki/Small_nuclear_RNA "Small nuclear RNA") contain unique 5′-caps. Sm-class snRNAs are found with 5′-trimethylguanosine caps, while Lsm-class snRNAs are found with 5′-monomethylphosphate caps.[[10]](#cite_note-matera2007-10)

In [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), and potentially also in higher organisms, some RNAs are capped with [NAD+](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide"), [NADH](/wiki/NADH "NADH"), or [3′-dephospho-coenzyme A](/wiki/Coenzyme_A "Coenzyme A").[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In all organisms, mRNA molecules can be decapped in a process known as [messenger RNA decapping](/wiki/Messenger_RNA_decapping "Messenger RNA decapping"). This is usually followed by degradation of the mRNA.

## Capping process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-prime_cap&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Capping process")]

The starting point for capping with 7-methylguanylate is the unaltered 5′ end of an RNA molecule, which terminates at a triphosphate group. This features a final nucleotide followed by three phosphate groups attached to the 5′ carbon.[[3]](#cite_note-shatkin-3) The capping process is initiated before the completion of transcription, as the nascent pre-mRNA is being synthesized.

1. One of the terminal phosphate groups is removed by [RNA triphosphatase](/wiki/RNA_triphosphatase "RNA triphosphatase"), leaving a bisphosphate group (i.e. 5′(ppN)[pN]n);
2. [GTP](/wiki/Guanosine_triphosphate "Guanosine triphosphate") is added to the terminal bisphosphate by [mRNA guanylyltransferase](/wiki/MRNA_guanylyltransferase "MRNA guanylyltransferase"), losing a [pyrophosphate](/wiki/Pyrophosphate "Pyrophosphate") from the GTP substrate in the process. This results in the 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage, producing 5′(Gp)(ppN)[pN]n;
3. The 7-nitrogen of guanine is methylated by [mRNA (guanine-*N*7-)-methyltransferase](/wiki/MRNA_(guanine-N7-)-methyltransferase "MRNA (guanine-N7-)-methyltransferase"), with [*S*-adenosyl-L-methionine](/wiki/S-Adenosyl_methionine "S-Adenosyl methionine") being demethylated to produce [*S*-adenosyl-L-homocysteine](/wiki/S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine "S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine"), resulting in 5′(m7Gp)(ppN)[pN]n (cap-0);
4. Cap-adjacent modifications can occur, normally to the first and second nucleotides, producing up to 5′(m7Gp)(ppN\*)(pN\*)[pN]n (cap-1 and cap-2);[[8]](#cite_note-fechter2005-8)
5. If the nearest cap-adjacent nucleotide is [2′-*O*-ribose methyl-adenosine](/wiki/2%27-O-methylation "2'-O-methylation") (i.e. 5′(m7Gp)(ppAm)[pN]n), it can be further methylated at the N6 methyl position to form [*N*6-methyladenosine](/wiki/N6-Methyladenosine "N6-Methyladenosine"), resulting in 5′(m7Gp)(ppm6Am)[pN]n.[[3]](#cite_note-shatkin-3)

The mechanism of capping with NAD+, NADH, or 3′-dephospho-coenzyme A is different. Capping with NAD+, NADH, or 3′-dephospho-coenzyme A is accomplished through an "ab initio capping mechanism," in which NAD+, NADH, or 3′-desphospho-coenzyme A serves as a "non-canonical initiating nucleotide" (NCIN) for [transcription initiation](/wiki/Transcription_(genetics) "Transcription (genetics)") by [RNA polymerase](/wiki/RNA_polymerase "RNA polymerase") and thereby directly is incorporated into the RNA product.[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11) Both bacterial RNA polymerase and eukaryotic [RNA polymerase II](/wiki/RNA_polymerase_II "RNA polymerase II") are able to carry out this "ab initio capping mechanism".[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11)

## Targeting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-prime_cap&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Targeting")]

For capping with 7-methylguanylate, the [capping enzyme](/wiki/Capping_enzyme "Capping enzyme") complex (CEC) binds to [RNA polymerase II](/wiki/RNA_polymerase_II "RNA polymerase II") before transcription starts. As soon as the 5′ end of the new transcript emerges from RNA polymerase II, the CEC carries out the capping process (this kind of mechanism ensures capping, as with [polyadenylation](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation")).[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-fabrega-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-hirose-16) The enzymes for capping can only bind to [RNA polymerase II](/wiki/RNA_polymerase_II "RNA polymerase II"), ensuring specificity to only these transcripts, which are almost entirely mRNA.[[14]](#cite_note-fabrega-14)[[16]](#cite_note-hirose-16)

Capping with NAD+, NADH, or 3′-dephospho-coenzyme A is targeted by [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(genetics) "Promoter (genetics)") sequence.[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11) Capping with NAD+, NADH, or 3′-dephospho-coenzyme A occurs only at promoters that have certain sequences at and immediately upstream of the transcription start site and therefore occurs only for RNAs synthesized from certain promoters.[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Five-prime_cap&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Function")]

The 5′ cap has four main functions:

1. Regulation of nuclear export;[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
2. Prevention of degradation by [exonucleases](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease");[[11]](#cite_note-PMID27383794-11)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)
3. Promotion of translation (see [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") and [translation](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)"));[[3]](#cite_note-shatkin-3)[[4]](#cite_note-banerjee-4)[[5]](#cite_note-sonenberg-5)
4. Promotion of 5′ proximal intron excision.[[22]](#cite_note-konarska1984-22)

Nuclear export of RNA is regulated by the [cap binding complex](/wiki/Cap_binding_complex "Cap binding complex") (CBC), which binds exclusively to 7-methylguanylate-capped RNA. The CBC is then recognized by the [nuclear pore complex](/wiki/Nuclear_pore_complex "Nuclear pore complex") and exported. Once in the cytoplasm after the pioneer round of translation, the CBC is replaced by the translation factors [eIF4E](/wiki/EIF4E "EIF4E") and [eIF4G](/wiki/EIF4G "EIF4G") of the [eIF4F](/wiki/EIF4F "EIF4F") complex.[[6]](#cite_note-marcotrigiano-6) This complex is then recognized by other translation initiation machinery including the ribosome.[[23]](#cite_note-Kapp2004-23)

Capping with 7-methylguanylate prevents 5′ degradation in two ways. First, degradation of the mRNA by 5′ exonucleases is prevented (as mentioned above) by functionally looking like a 3′ end. Second, the CBC and eIF4E/eIF4G block the access of decapping enzymes to the cap. This increases the [half-life](/wiki/Half-life "Half-life") of the mRNA, essential in eukaryotes as the export and translation processes take significant time.

Decapping of a 7-methylguanylate-capped mRNA is catalyzed by the decapping complex made up of at least Dcp1 and Dcp2, which must compete with eIF4E to bind the cap. Thus the 7-methylguanylate cap is a marker of an actively translating mRNA and is used by cells to regulate mRNA half-lives in response to new stimuli. Undesirable mRNAs are sent to [P-bodies](/wiki/P-bodies "P-bodies") for temporary storage or decapping, the details of which are still being resolved.[[24]](#cite_note-Parker2007-24)

The mechanism of 5′ proximal intron excision promotion is not well understood, but the 7-methylguanylate cap appears to loop around and interact with the [spliceosome](/wiki/Spliceosome "Spliceosome") in the splicing process, promoting intron excision.
